policy_statement: Significantly expand Climate Resilient Farming (CRF) program funding to help farmers implement alternative manure management systems that reduce methane emissions.
mechanism_description: The policy expands program funding to assist farmers in adopting alternative manure management systems, reducing methane emissions from manure.
primary_causal_pathway: Agricultural practice change (manure handling) → ↓ anaerobic decomposition and/or ↑ methane capture → ↓ CH₄ emissions → ↓ radiative forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed activity/system: On-farm manure collection, storage, and treatment systems are upgraded or replaced through financial/technical support (e.g., covered storage, solid–liquid separation, composting/aeration, anaerobic digesters with gas capture/use).  
2) Mediating process: Reduced oxygen-free (anaerobic) conditions that generate methane and/or capture of produced biogas for flaring or energy use.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower net methane emissions from manure management (and sometimes additional CO₂e reductions if captured biogas displaces fossil energy), leading to reduced GHG-driven warming.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing agricultural methane emissions by changing manure management practices and/or capturing methane before it is emitted.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Manure system interventions primarily act by reducing or capturing methane, directly lowering GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While there can be co-benefits (energy generation, odor/water-quality improvements, farm resilience), the core and largest climate effect across policies using this mechanism is CH₄ abatement, which is mitigation rather than adaptation/resource efficiency/NBS.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a specific program framed as “resilient farming” mainly targets flood management or nutrient runoff, it could lean adaptation; however, this canonical mechanism explicitly targets alternative manure management to reduce methane, keeping mitigation dominant.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy that supports alternative manure management specifically to reduce methane shares this same primary mitigation pathway (CH₄ reduction).  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether the policy is best tagged later as “agriculture,” “waste-to-energy,” “renewable gas,” or “air quality co-benefits” depends on policy-level details (e.g., digesters vs composting, energy export, verification), and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If implementation relies primarily on anaerobic digestion for energy export, some of the benefit may come from fossil fuel displacement (still mitigation) and could introduce leakage/combustion accounting issues; if manure is shifted to systems increasing N₂O, net GHG benefits could be reduced—policy design and measurement determine net outcomes, but the primary intended pathway remains CH₄ mitigation.
key_indicators: "Expand funding"; "CRF program funding"; "assist farmers"; "implementing alternative manure management practice systems"; "reduce methane emissions"
co_benefits: Potential water quality improvements and reduced nutrient runoff/odor from improved manure management (not explicitly stated).
instance_edge_case_notes: No specific instrument (grant vs loan vs rebate) is specified beyond “expand funding,” so it is classified at the general incentive-program level.
